attributes.stem:
A 56-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with worsening of the 'blood blisters' surrounding her mouth. She has had one or two similar smaller lesions for several years, however has noticed in the last three months a dramatic increase in number and size. She has been camouflaging these with lipstick but is increasingly concerned about their appearance.

On further history, she has been experiencing symptoms of general fatigue, lightheadedness, and dyspnoea on exertion. The only significant aspects of her medical history are recurrent nosebleeds in childhood and occasional passage of black and tarry bowel motions.

She thinks her parent may have had similar spots on their lips, although not nearly as many. They died at age 65 from a haemorrhagic stroke.

On examination, she appears generally pale with conjunctival pallor. She has multiple vascular lesions on her lips and tongue. There are scattered telangiectasias across her nails and palmar surfaces. There are no other similar lesions noticed elsewhere. Her pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Abdominal palpation is unremarkable, however on rectal examination melaena is detected.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This is a case of hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT), also known as Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome (named after Sir William Osler, Henri Jules Louis Marie Rendu, and Frederick Parkes Weber, who described it in the late 19th and early 20th centuries).

HHT is an autosomal dominant genetic disorder characterised by vascular dysplasia; mucocutaneous and gastrointestinal telangiectasias are associated with arteriovenous malformations in the lungs, liver or brain. Most of the lesions are asymptomatic but about 25% do give rise to problems such as chronic anaemia due to insidious bleeding from the malformations in the digestive tract. Recurrent nosebleeds occur in up to 90% of affected individuals. Though it often shows in childhood, symptoms may start at any age.

Curaçao HHT diagnostic criteria:

  1. Epistaxis

  2. Telangiectasias

  3. Visceral fast-flow lesions

  4. Family history

Diagnosis is considered definite with three or more criteria present, possible with two criteria present, and unlikely with only one criterion present.

Multidisciplinary treatment is primarily symptomatic - regular checks of oximetry and blood count for anaemia or polycythaemia; iron supplementation and transfusion for anaemia; nasal humidification, lubricants and haemostatic products for nasal bleeding. Laser can be used for cutaneous, nasal or gastrointestinal telangiectasias.

Whilst eponymous names are difficult to remember, eponymous and scientific names are used interchangeably in everyday practice and attempt should be made to have knowledge of both.

Sturge-Weber syndrome is a non-inherited congenital neurocutaneous disorder characterised by port-wine stain and capillary-venous malformations in the brain and eyes, as well as seizures and neurological abnormalities. It is also known as meningofacial angiomatosis. These lesions would be present at birth and whilst may enlarge over time, and thus does not the timeline of this scenario.

Peutz-Jeghers syndrome is an autosomal dominant disease characterised by gastrointestinal polyps in association with pigmentation affecting skin and mucous membranes. Pigmentation usually develops before age 5. Individuals with PJS are 15 times more likely to develop internal cancer compared with the general population. The overall risk of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome patients developing a cancer over adult life is 93%. The pigmentation however appears as flat pigmented spots inside or around the mouth which are benign, and tend to resolve in adulthood rather than appear.

Stevens-Johnson syndrome is a rare, acute, serious, and potentially fatal skin reaction in which there are sheet-like skin and mucosal loss; most often secondary to a drug exposure. It exists on a spectrum with toxic epidermal necrolysis. The time frame is much more acute in hours to days rather than years.

Klippel–Trénaunay syndrome is a rare congenital vascular bone syndrome characterised by a triad of signs: port-wine stain, venous malformations (varicose veins), limb hypertrophy often unilaterally of the lower limb up to 2 centimeters. The varicose veins may be more obvious later in life (adolescence) and can bleeding from the rectum as well. However there is no sign of limb hypertrophy, sindactyly or polydactyly in this case.
learningPoints: Hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia should be considered with frequent and persistent epistaxis
Fatigue and dyspnoea may be subtle signs of anaemia
references: Dompmartin A, Revencu N, Boon LM, Vikkula M. Disorders Affecting Cutaneous Vasculature. In: Griffiths C, Barker J, Creamer D, Bleiker TO, Chalmers R, editors. Rook's Textbook of Dermatology. United Kingdon: Blackwell, Wiley; 2016. p1939-4339

McAsey A, Gossage JR. Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia. In National Organization for Rare Disorders. 2021. Accessed 28 Apr 2021, from: https://rarediseases.org/rare-diseases/hereditary-hemorrhagic-telangiectasia/
